nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear, slow-to-moderately-flowing tributary streams and pools in the Western Ghats foothills; water is typically shallow, well-oxygenated, and shaded by riparian vegetation. Found in Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu in river systems including the Meenachil, Bharatapuzha, Chalakkudy, and Sita.

decorPreferences: dense surface or emergent vegetation (floating plants, water sprite) for shade and cover
fine-leaved stem plants for egg deposition
gentle surface agitation via sponge or air-powered filter
tight-fitting lid — essential; this species is a confirmed jumper

diet.notes: Micro surface predator feeding primarily on small aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates in the wild. In the aquarium, readily accepts small live and frozen foods (Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, grindal worms, micro-worms, fruit flies) as the main diet. Also takes suitably sized dried foods (small flakes, micro-pellets) but benefits from regular live or frozen variety. Feed at the surface; sinking foods are mostly ignored.

goodWith: ember tetras and other small tetras
avoidWith: large or predatory fish that may eat them
aggressive or nippy species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
boisterous or fast-moving tankmates that outcompete at the surface
very small invertebrates (cherry shrimp fry may be eaten)

summary: A tiny, hatchet-bodied surface danio from India’s Western Ghats. The laterally-compressed body mimics true hatchetfishes (Gasteropelecidae) but the two are unrelated. Peaceful and easy to keep in well-planted tanks, though an absolute jumper — a tight lid is non-negotiable.
careNotes:
The Orange Hatchet Danio is a small (2.5–3.5 cm), laterally compressed schooling fish endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India. Despite its common name and hatchet-shaped profile, it is not related to the true hatchetfishes (family Gasteropelecidae of South America) — the body shape is a convergent adaptation for surface dwelling. It belongs instead to family Danionidae.

This species inhabits the upper water column and spends the majority of its time near the surface, darting to intercept small insects and invertebrates. A group of at least 8 specimens is strongly recommended to produce natural schooling behaviour and reduce stress; larger groups of 12–20 are even better.

Escape risk is high. Dadios are confirmed jumpers and will launch themselves through any gap in the lid, particularly when startled. A fully sealed or very tightly fitting cover is non-negotiable — even brief periods with the lid open during maintenance have cost aquarists entire schools.

Water conditions should reflect their native Western Ghats hill-stream origin: soft to moderately hard (gH 2–12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), and relatively cool (20–26°C). Temperatures above 27°C long-term are stressful. Gentle filtration with moderate surface agitation is appropriate; strong direct flow is unnecessary. Partial water changes of 20–30% weekly help maintain quality.

A planted tank with floating or emergent surface vegetation (water sprite, Indian fern, hornwort) provides the shade and security this species requires. A dark substrate and subdued lighting enhance the fish’s orange and silver coloration. The species is entirely freshwater; brackish or marine conditions are not applicable.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner requiring no parental care. Conditioning on live foods (Daphnia, Artemia, grindal worms) triggers spawning. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or can be deposited over a yarn mop. Adults eat eggs readily, so spawning adults should be removed or a breeding mesh separator used. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at 24°C; fry become free-swimming after an additional 4–5 days. Initial food must be infusoria or commercial first-fry food (Sera Micron, APBreed); transition to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 7–10 days. Breeding difficulty is moderate — straightforward conditioning but fry require infusoria-grade foods and are extremely sensitive to water quality.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Laubuka dadiburjori
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (20–30 mm SL), temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–179 ppm / approx. gH 2–10), minimum tank dimensions (90×30 cm), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates, dried foods accepted), native range (Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu; rivers Meenachil, Bharatapuzha, Chalakkudy, Sita), schooling (8–10 minimum), egg-scattering breeding, escape risk (jumper, tight lid required), and biotope notes. Recognises synonyms Laubuca dadiburjori, Chela dadiburjori, Neochela dadiburjori.

additional.name: FishBase — Laubuka dadiburjori (Dadio)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 2.5 cm SL male, to 3.0 cm female), temperature (22–24°C), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Tamil Nadu to Goa, India), habitat (streams and pools, freshwater pelagic). Family confirmed as Danionidae, not Cyprinidae sensu stricto, consistent with modern classification.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Orange Hatchet Danio
additional.notes: Corroborated adult size (up to 1.5 in / ~3.8 cm), temperature (65–85°F / 18–29°C; preferred 75°F / 24°C), pH (6.0–7.8), minimum tank (20 gal / ~76 L for a school of 6+), surface-dwelling schooling behaviour, jumping risk, carnivorous diet preference, egg-scattering breeding (up to 30 eggs per spawn). Size upper bound slightly higher than SeriouslyFish; used SeriouslyFish/FishBase consensus range of 3.5 cm max.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; conditioning on live foods required. Adults eat eggs immediately — use breeding mesh or remove adults post-spawn. Fry need infusoria initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 7–10 days. Eggs hatch in ~48–72 hours; fry free-swimming within 4–5 additional days.
